clinicalStem: A 7-year-old unvaccinated boy is brought to the primary health care centre with a 3-day history of low-grade fever, sore throat, and a hoarse voice that is worsening. His mother reports he has had difficulty swallowing since yesterday. On examination, temperature is 38.1°C, HR 110 bpm, RR 24/min, and SpO₂ 94% on room air. He has a 'bull-neck' appearance from cervical lymphadenopathy and soft tissue oedema. Inspection of the oropharynx reveals a tough greyish-white membrane firmly adherent to the tonsils and posterior pharynx that bleeds when attempts are made to remove it. Breath sounds reveal inspiratory stridor. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate immediate action?
options: Administer diphtheria antitoxin (DAT) intravenously after a sensitivity test
Give oral erythromycin and arrange outpatient ENT review
Secure the airway and arrange urgent transfer to a facility capable of intubation or tracheostomy
Start intravenous benzylpenicillin and observe in the clinic for 4 hours
Perform throat swab for culture and await results before initiating treatment
explanation: This child has classic diphtheria with a pseudomembrane causing progressive upper airway obstruction, evidenced by stridor and falling oxygen saturation. The immediate priority is airway security; definitive airway management (intubation or tracheostomy by an experienced team) must not be delayed. DAT and antibiotics are essential but cannot be given safely before the airway is secured. Transferring while maintaining airway patency takes precedence over any other intervention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: DAT is a critical treatment but administering it without first addressing the compromised airway risks complete obstruction during the procedure — airway comes first.
Oral antibiotics and outpatient review are wholly inadequate for a child with stridor and SpO₂ 94%; this approach risks fatal airway obstruction.
Correct — securing the airway is the single most urgent priority when a patient with diphtheria develops stridor and hypoxia.
IV penicillin is part of management but does not reverse existing membrane-related obstruction quickly enough; airway protection must precede antibiotic administration.
Awaiting culture results before treatment is dangerous in a clinical diagnosis of diphtheria with airway compromise; treatment must not be delayed for microbiological confirmation.
clinicalPearl: In diphtheria with stridor, the airway is the immediate priority — DAT and antibiotics follow, never precede, securing ventilation.
keyClue: Stridor + SpO₂ 94% + adherent grey pharyngeal membrane in an unvaccinated child = impending airway obstruction.
commonExamTrap: Choosing DAT first because it is the 'specific' treatment — correct in principle, but airway takes absolute priority.
missedConcept: ABCDE prioritisation: airway obstruction is a life-threatening emergency that overrides all other specific treatments.
redFlagReason: Progressive upper airway obstruction from a diphtheric pseudomembrane can cause complete asphyxia within minutes; failure to secure the airway is immediately fatal.

clinicalStem: A 10-year-old girl was diagnosed with pharyngeal diphtheria 10 days ago and received diphtheria antitoxin plus a 14-day course of erythromycin. She appeared to be recovering but now presents to the primary health centre with palpitations, dizziness, and two episodes of near-syncope. On examination, pulse is 45 bpm and irregular. An ECG shows complete heart block with a ventricular escape rate. There is no stridor, and her throat appears healing. What is the most appropriate next step in her management?
options: Increase the dose of erythromycin and reassure the family that arrhythmias resolve spontaneously
Administer a repeat dose of diphtheria antitoxin to neutralise remaining exotoxin
Arrange urgent transfer to a hospital for cardiac monitoring and possible temporary pacing
Start oral prednisolone to reduce myocardial inflammation and review in 48 hours
Perform echocardiography at the clinic and initiate digoxin for rate control
explanation: Diphtheritic myocarditis typically presents 1–2 weeks after onset and is the leading cause of death from diphtheria. Complete heart block with syncopal episodes is a haemodynamic emergency requiring urgent hospital transfer for continuous cardiac monitoring and, if haemodynamically compromised, temporary cardiac pacing. Antitoxin is no longer useful once exotoxin has bound to cardiac tissue. Digoxin is contraindicated in complete heart block.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antibiotics treat the organism and prevent further toxin production but do not reverse cardiac toxin damage already done; reassurance is dangerously wrong here.
DAT neutralises free toxin only; once exotoxin has bound to myocardial cells, repeat DAT has no benefit and carries risk of anaphylaxis.
Correct — complete heart block with near-syncope is an emergency requiring hospital-level cardiac monitoring and likely temporary pacing.
Steroids are not proven to reduce diphtheritic myocarditis severity; reviewing in 48 hours risks fatal asystole in a patient with complete heart block.
Echocardiography is useful but not available in primary care; digoxin is absolutely contraindicated in complete heart block and could precipitate asystole.
clinicalPearl: Diphtheritic myocarditis peaks at 1–2 weeks post-infection; complete heart block is a life-threatening complication requiring urgent pacing, and digoxin is contraindicated.
keyClue: Complete heart block + near-syncope occurring 10 days into diphtheria illness = diphtheritic myocarditis emergency.
commonExamTrap: Giving a repeat dose of antitoxin — it is useless against cell-bound toxin and risks anaphylaxis.
missedConcept: Diphtheria exotoxin irreversibly inhibits protein synthesis in cardiac myocytes; antitoxin only neutralises circulating (unbound) toxin.
redFlagReason: Complete heart block with near-syncope can degenerate to asystole without pacing; this is a cardiac emergency.

topic: Diphtheria — Post-exposure prophylaxis and contact management
clinicalStem: A confirmed case of pharyngeal diphtheria is identified in an 8-year-old child in a primary school. The public health team contacts the primary care clinic to guide management of close contacts. Among the contacts is a 35-year-old teacher who received childhood primary immunisation but has no record of a booster in the last 10 years. She is currently asymptomatic with a normal throat examination. Throat swab has been taken. What is the most appropriate prophylactic management for this teacher?
options: Diphtheria antitoxin IM plus erythromycin for 7 days
A single dose of tetanus-diphtheria (Td) booster vaccine only; no antibiotics required
Erythromycin for 7–10 days plus a diphtheria-containing booster vaccine if her last booster was more than 5 years ago
Reassure and monitor — adults with previous vaccination do not need prophylaxis
Isolate the teacher for 14 days and await throat swab results before any intervention
explanation: Close contacts of diphtheria cases should receive antibiotic prophylaxis (erythromycin 7–10 days or benzylpenicillin IM as single dose) regardless of vaccination status, and a booster vaccine if the last diphtheria-containing vaccine was more than 5 years ago. Vaccination alone is insufficient because antibiotics are needed to eradicate possible nasopharyngeal carriage. Antitoxin is reserved for symptomatic cases, not asymptomatic contacts.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Antitoxin is indicated for active disease, not asymptomatic prophylaxis; giving it to contacts is not recommended and risks sensitisation.
Vaccine alone does not eradicate carriage; antibiotic prophylaxis is always required for close contacts.
Correct — antibiotic prophylaxis plus booster vaccination (if >5 years since last dose) is the standard approach for close contacts.
Even vaccinated adults can carry C. diphtheriae; prophylaxis is indicated for all close contacts irrespective of immune status.
Awaiting results before treatment may allow propagation of infection; prophylaxis should begin immediately for high-risk contacts.
clinicalPearl: All diphtheria close contacts need antibiotic prophylaxis (erythromycin 7–10 days) and a booster vaccine if more than 5 years since last dose, regardless of prior vaccination.
keyClue: Asymptomatic close contact + last diphtheria booster >10 years ago = antibiotics AND booster vaccine both required.
commonExamTrap: Giving antitoxin to asymptomatic contacts — antitoxin is a treatment for active disease, not prophylaxis.
missedConcept: Vaccination confers immunity against toxin effects but does not prevent nasopharyngeal carriage; antibiotics are essential to eradicate carriage in contacts.

subtopic: First-line treatment and route of administration
clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the primary health care centre 10 minutes after eating a peanut-containing snack. She reports throat tightness, difficulty breathing, and feels faint. On examination: BP 78/50 mmHg, HR 128 bpm, RR 26/min, SpO₂ 91%. She has generalised urticaria, angioedema of the lips, and audible wheeze bilaterally. She has no known cardiac history. Which of the following is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Administer IV chlorphenamine 10 mg slowly
Give IV hydrocortisone 200 mg immediately
Administer adrenaline (epinephrine) 0.5 mg IM (1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh
Establish IV access and give 500 mL of 0.9% saline as a fluid challenge
Apply a high-flow oxygen mask at 15 L/min and reassess in 5 minutes
explanation: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) into the anterolateral thigh is the first-line and most critical treatment for anaphylaxis in adults. It reverses bronchospasm, vasodilatation, and angioedema simultaneously. The IM route (anterolateral thigh) achieves faster and more reliable plasma levels than the deltoid or subcutaneous route. All other treatments (antihistamines, steroids, fluids, oxygen) are adjuncts and must not precede or delay adrenaline.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Chlorphenamine (antihistamine) is an adjunct; it does not reverse bronchospasm or shock and must not be given before adrenaline.
Hydrocortisone is an adjunct that takes hours to act and does not reverse acute anaphylaxis; it must not replace or precede adrenaline.
Correct — IM adrenaline 1:1000, 0.5 mg into the anterolateral thigh is the immediate, definitive life-saving treatment.
IV fluids address hypotension as an adjunct but do not reverse bronchospasm or angioedema; adrenaline must come first.
High-flow oxygen is important adjunct therapy but does not treat the underlying pathophysiology of anaphylaxis; adrenaline is the priority.
clinicalPearl: In anaphylaxis, IM adrenaline into the anterolateral thigh is the single most important first intervention — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts only.
keyClue: Urticaria + angioedema + wheeze + hypotension + tachycardia after allergen exposure = anaphylaxis requiring immediate IM adrenaline.
commonExamTrap: Giving antihistamine or hydrocortisone first because they are 'safer' — this delay can be fatal.
missedConcept: Adrenaline 1:1000 IM (not IV, not subcutaneous) into the anterolateral thigh is the route and dose for anaphylaxis in primary care.
redFlagReason: Anaphylaxis with hypotension, bronchospasm, and angioedema is immediately life-threatening; delay in adrenaline administration causes preventable death.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman attends the primary care clinic every spring with sneezing, bilateral watery nasal discharge, nasal blockage, and itchy eyes lasting 6–8 weeks. Symptoms interfere with her sleep and work performance. She has tried oral loratadine (non-sedating antihistamine) for the past two seasons with only partial relief of sneezing and itch but persistent nasal blockage. She has no asthma or nasal polyps. Skin prick testing is positive for grass pollen. What is the most appropriate next step in her management?
options: Switch to a sedating antihistamine such as chlorphenamine for better symptom control
Add an intranasal corticosteroid (e.g., fluticasone nasal spray) to her current loratadine
Prescribe a short course of oral prednisolone 30 mg daily for 5 days
Refer immediately for immunotherapy without trying additional pharmacotherapy
Prescribe a topical nasal decongestant (oxymetazoline) for long-term use
explanation: Intranasal corticosteroids (INCS) are the most effective pharmacotherapy for moderate-to-severe allergic rhinitis and are particularly superior for nasal congestion, which antihistamines address poorly. When antihistamines alone provide inadequate control, INCS should be added as the next step before considering systemic steroids or referral for immunotherapy. They have an excellent safety profile with minimal systemic absorption.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Sedating antihistamines offer no advantage over non-sedating ones for nasal congestion and impair cognitive function; they are not recommended as an upgrade.
Correct — adding an intranasal corticosteroid is the evidence-based next step when antihistamines give incomplete control, especially for nasal blockage.
Oral prednisolone is reserved for very severe, refractory cases; it is not appropriate as a second-line step when INCS has not yet been tried.
Allergen immunotherapy is considered after pharmacotherapy fails; it should not be offered before optimising first-line and second-line drug treatment.
Oxymetazoline causes rhinitis medicamentosa if used for more than 3–5 days; it is not recommended for long-term management.
clinicalPearl: Intranasal corticosteroids are the most effective single agent for allergic rhinitis, especially nasal congestion — add them when antihistamines give incomplete relief.
keyClue: Persistent nasal blockage despite non-sedating antihistamine = intranasal corticosteroid is the next step.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing oral prednisolone as the second step — INCS must be tried before systemic steroids.
missedConcept: Antihistamines control sneezing/itch/rhinorrhoea but are relatively ineffective for nasal congestion; INCS address the full symptom spectrum.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old man with a documented penicillin allergy (described as a widespread itchy rash after amoxicillin 15 years ago with no anaphylaxis, angioedema, or systemic features) presents with community-acquired pneumonia requiring antibiotic treatment. He has no other drug allergies. Sputum culture results are pending. His vital signs are stable and he does not require hospital admission. Which of the following antibiotic choices is most appropriate at this stage?
options: Amoxicillin-clavulanate — the original reaction was mild so cross-reactivity risk is negligible
A macrolide antibiotic (e.g., clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily) as first-line empirical therapy
Refer to an allergist for formal penicillin skin testing before initiating any antibiotic
Meropenem intravenously — carbapenems have no cross-reactivity with penicillin
Oral doxycycline — safer choice than macrolides in penicillin-allergic patients
explanation: In a patient with a non-severe penicillin allergy history (delayed maculopapular rash without systemic features), a macrolide such as clarithromycin is the appropriate empirical alternative for community-acquired pneumonia in primary care. It covers the typical and atypical organisms responsible for CAP and avoids the beta-lactam class. Beta-lactams (including amoxicillin-clavulanate) should not be given without formal allergy assessment. Meropenem IV is inappropriate for outpatient CAP.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin-clavulanate contains a beta-lactam ring; even though cross-reactivity is lower than historically believed, prescribing without allergy assessment in a documented penicillin-allergic patient is not appropriate in primary care.
Correct — a macrolide is the standard empirical alternative for CAP in penicillin-allergic outpatients and provides adequate coverage.
Referral for allergy testing is appropriate long-term but not before starting antibiotics in active pneumonia; treatment must not be delayed.
Meropenem is an IV carbapenem reserved for hospital use; it is inappropriate for outpatient CAP.
Doxycycline is a reasonable alternative for atypical CAP but is generally considered second-line to macrolides in this setting; option B is more appropriate as first choice.
clinicalPearl: For penicillin-allergic patients with community-acquired pneumonia, a macrolide (clarithromycin/azithromycin) is the standard outpatient alternative.
keyClue: Non-severe penicillin allergy history + CAP not requiring admission = macrolide antibiotic.
commonExamTrap: Using amoxicillin-clavulanate because the original reaction was 'mild' — allergy labelling requires proper assessment before re-challenge.
missedConcept: Macrolides cover both typical and atypical CAP organisms and are the go-to alternative when penicillins are contraindicated in outpatient settings.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents with recurrent, widespread, itchy raised wheals appearing and disappearing at different body sites over the past 8 weeks. Individual lesions last less than 24 hours and resolve without bruising or skin changes. She denies any lip or tongue swelling, breathing difficulty, or joint pain. There is no clear dietary or environmental trigger. Full blood count, thyroid function tests, and ESR are all normal. Dermographism is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Refer urgently to dermatology for skin biopsy to exclude vasculitis
Start a non-sedating H1 antihistamine at standard dose and review in 2–4 weeks
Prescribe a 2-week course of oral prednisolone 30 mg daily and taper
Order a comprehensive allergy panel including RAST testing for 30 common allergens
Initiate omalizumab (anti-IgE therapy) as first-line biologic treatment
explanation: This presentation is consistent with chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU), defined as urticaria persisting for more than 6 weeks without an identifiable exogenous cause. First-line management is a non-sedating H1 antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) at licensed doses for at least 4 weeks. The majority of patients respond adequately. Routine allergy testing in CSU has very low diagnostic yield. Steroids are reserved for acute severe episodes, not chronic management. Omalizumab is third-line after antihistamines and anti-leukotrienes fail.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urgent dermatology referral for biopsy is not warranted when lesions are transient (<24 h), non-bruising, and blood work is normal; vasculitic urticaria is unlikely here.
Correct — non-sedating H1 antihistamine is the evidence-based first-line treatment for chronic spontaneous urticaria.
Systemic steroids are not recommended for chronic urticaria management; they are used short-term only for acute severe flares.
Extensive allergy panel testing in CSU is not recommended as a routine investigation — it is costly, has poor yield, and rarely changes management.
Omalizumab is a third-line biologic reserved for antihistamine-refractory CSU; it is not appropriate as first-line therapy.
clinicalPearl: Chronic spontaneous urticaria (>6 weeks) is treated step-wise: non-sedating H1 antihistamine first, then up-dose, then add anti-leukotriene, then omalizumab.
keyClue: Wheals lasting <24 hours at different sites for >6 weeks with normal investigations = chronic spontaneous urticaria; start non-sedating antihistamine.
commonExamTrap: Ordering extensive allergy panels — they are rarely diagnostic in CSU and are not recommended as routine first investigations.
missedConcept: In CSU, lesions that leave no residual skin change and resolve within 24 hours do not need biopsy; vasculitic urticaria leaves pigmented lesions and lesions last >24 hours.

topic: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus — Recognition and initial workup
clinicalStem: A 27-year-old woman presents to the primary care clinic with a 4-month history of intermittent joint pain affecting her small hand joints, fatigue, and recurrent oral ulcers. Over the past 3 weeks she has developed a butterfly-shaped rash across her cheeks and nose that worsens after sun exposure. She reports losing clumps of hair in the shower. Blood pressure is 138/88 mmHg. Urinalysis shows 2+ protein and 1+ blood. What is the single most important initial investigation to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies
Antinuclear antibody (ANA) titre
Complement levels (C3 and C4) alone
Skin biopsy of the malar rash
Anti-dsDNA antibodies as the sole confirmatory test
explanation: ANA is the best initial screening test for SLE, with a sensitivity exceeding 95% — a negative ANA makes SLE very unlikely. It should be the first serological test ordered when SLE is suspected. If ANA is positive, more specific antibodies (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm) are then ordered. Anti-dsDNA is highly specific but not sufficiently sensitive as a standalone first-line test. RF/anti-CCP are for rheumatoid arthritis. Skin biopsy is not the first step.
wrongAnswerExplanations: RF and anti-CCP are used to investigate rheumatoid arthritis; while joint involvement overlaps, they are not the appropriate initial test when SLE is suspected.
Correct — ANA is the first-line screening test for SLE; its high sensitivity means a negative result largely excludes SLE.
Complement levels (C3/C4) can be low in active SLE but are non-specific and not used for initial diagnosis; they monitor disease activity.
Skin biopsy is not a first-line investigation; clinical and serological criteria establish the diagnosis in most cases.
Anti-dsDNA is highly specific for SLE but has moderate sensitivity; it is ordered after a positive ANA, not as the first test.
clinicalPearl: ANA is the screening test for SLE (sensitivity >95%); a positive result prompts anti-dsDNA and anti-Sm testing for confirmation.
keyClue: Butterfly rash + photosensitivity + oral ulcers + alopecia + proteinuria/haematuria in a young woman = SLE; first test is ANA.
commonExamTrap: Ordering anti-dsDNA first — it is confirmatory but not a good screening test due to lower sensitivity; ANA comes first.
missedConcept: ANA negativity virtually excludes SLE; if ANA is negative, the diagnosis should be reconsidered before ordering more specific antibodies.

clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman has been diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis after presenting with a 3-month history of bilateral, symmetrical swelling and morning stiffness of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints lasting more than 1 hour. RF and anti-CCP are both strongly positive. ESR is 68 mm/hr. X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia but no erosions yet. She has no hepatic, renal, or pulmonary comorbidity. Which of the following represents the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying strategy?
options: Initiate a biologic agent (e.g., adalimumab) immediately given her high-risk serology
Prescribe naproxen 500 mg twice daily and review in 3 months before starting a DMARD
Start methotrexate with folic acid supplementation and monitor with regular blood tests
Give a prolonged course of prednisolone 15 mg daily as bridge therapy before any DMARD
Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy is sufficient as she has no erosions yet
explanation: Methotrexate is the anchor DMARD and the established first-line disease-modifying treatment for rheumatoid arthritis. It should be initiated promptly after diagnosis to prevent joint erosion and disability. Folic acid 5 mg weekly is co-prescribed to reduce methotrexate toxicity. Regular monitoring (FBC, LFTs, renal function) is mandatory. Biologic agents are reserved for inadequate response to conventional DMARDs. Hydroxychloroquine monotherapy is insufficient for seropositive RA.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Biologic agents (e.g., anti-TNF) are second-line for patients who fail at least one conventional DMARD; they are not appropriate as initial therapy.
NSAIDs alone treat symptoms but do not modify disease course; delaying DMARD therapy allows joint destruction to progress — early DMARD initiation is essential.
Correct — methotrexate plus folic acid is the standard first-line DMARD for newly diagnosed RA and should be started promptly.
Long-term steroids are not an appropriate DMARD strategy; short-term bridging steroids may be used while waiting for DMARDs to take effect, but not as monotherapy.
Hydroxychloroquine is used in mild seronegative or very early RA; seropositive RA with elevated inflammatory markers warrants methotrexate, not hydroxychloroquine alone.
clinicalPearl: Methotrexate with folic acid is the anchor DMARD for RA; early initiation prevents erosive joint damage — never delay DMARD for symptom control alone.
keyClue: Seropositive RA (RF+, anti-CCP+) + elevated ESR + morning stiffness >1 hour = methotrexate as first DMARD.
commonExamTrap: Starting a biologic as first-line because the serology is 'high-risk' — biologics are reserved for DMARD failure.
missedConcept: DMARD therapy must begin at diagnosis, not after symptoms persist — early treatment prevents irreversible joint erosion.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain of 6 kg, constipation, and low mood. Examination reveals dry skin, periorbital puffiness, a slow relaxing ankle jerk reflex, and a non-tender diffusely enlarged thyroid gland. TSH is 42 mIU/L (normal 0.4–4.0 mIU/L), free T4 is 7.2 pmol/L (normal 12–22 pmol/L), and anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies are markedly elevated. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Refer to endocrinology before starting any treatment — autoimmune hypothyroidism requires specialist initiation
Start levothyroxine at full replacement dose (100–150 mcg daily) and recheck TSH in 6 months
Start levothyroxine at a low dose (25–50 mcg daily) and titrate based on TSH at 6–8 weeks
Prescribe selenium supplementation to reduce anti-TPO titres before starting levothyroxine
Advise dietary iodine restriction and repeat thyroid function tests in 3 months
explanation: Levothyroxine is the treatment of choice for autoimmune hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's thyroiditis). In a previously well adult without ischaemic heart disease, treatment is started at 25–50 mcg daily and titrated upward every 6–8 weeks based on TSH levels until the TSH normalises. Starting at full replacement dose risks precipitating palpitations or angina, particularly if there is unrecognised cardiac disease. Specialist referral is not required for uncomplicated hypothyroidism in primary care.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Specialist referral is not required for straightforward autoimmune hypothyroidism; primary care physicians routinely diagnose and manage this condition.
Starting at full replacement dose (100–150 mcg) is inappropriate in primary care without first ruling out ischaemic heart disease; it can precipitate arrhythmia or angina.
Correct — levothyroxine starting at 25–50 mcg with titration every 6–8 weeks guided by TSH is the safe, evidence-based approach.
Selenium may reduce anti-TPO antibody titres but does not treat hypothyroidism; it is not a substitute for levothyroxine.
Dietary iodine restriction is not standard management for Hashimoto's thyroiditis; levothyroxine replacement is the treatment.
clinicalPearl: Start levothyroxine at 25–50 mcg in adults, titrate every 6–8 weeks by TSH — never start at full replacement to avoid cardiac complications.
keyClue: Elevated TSH + low free T4 + high anti-TPO + goitre = Hashimoto's hypothyroidism requiring levothyroxine.
commonExamTrap: Starting at full replacement dose immediately — safe only in young, healthy individuals with no cardiac risk; always start low.
missedConcept: TSH-guided titration of levothyroxine every 6–8 weeks is the safest approach; over-replacement can cause atrial fibrillation and bone loss.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old male shepherd presents to the rural primary health centre with a 3-week history of undulant fever peaking in the evenings, drenching night sweats, diffuse arthralgia, fatigue, and low back pain. He regularly consumes unpasteurised goat cheese and has had direct contact with goat placentas during lambing season. Examination reveals mild hepatosplenomegaly. Temperature is 38.6°C, WBC 4.2×10⁹/L (lymphocytosis), haemoglobin 11.4 g/dL. Malaria rapid antigen test is negative. Which of the following investigations is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Blood cultures in standard aerobic bottles for 3 days
Serum agglutination test (SAT/Wright's test) with titres ≥1:160 considered diagnostic
Widal test to exclude typhoid fever as the primary differential
Bone marrow aspirate for culture as the gold-standard first-line test
Thick and thin blood films repeated over 72 hours
explanation: Brucellosis is strongly suspected in this shepherd with classic epidemiological risk factors (unpasteurised dairy, animal contact during parturition), undulant fever, and hepatosplenomegaly. The serum agglutination test (SAT/Wright's test) is the most widely available, practical, and validated serological test for brucellosis in primary care settings. A titre of ≥1:160 is considered significant. Blood cultures grow Brucella spp. but require prolonged incubation (up to 4 weeks in BACTEC) and are not the most practical first step. Bone marrow culture has higher sensitivity but is invasive and not first-line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Blood cultures can confirm brucellosis but require prolonged aerobic incubation (up to 4 weeks) and are not as practical as serology for initial confirmation in primary care.
Correct — SAT (Wright's test) at titres ≥1:160 is the standard serological test for brucellosis and is the most practical first-line confirmatory investigation.
The Widal test is used for typhoid (Salmonella typhi) and has no role in confirming brucellosis; this would not establish the diagnosis.
Bone marrow culture is the gold standard for sensitivity but is invasive and is not appropriate as a first-line test in primary care.
Thick and thin blood films detect parasites (malaria, trypanosomiasis) — the malaria RDT is already negative and blood films do not diagnose brucellosis.
clinicalPearl: In a patient with undulant fever and animal/dairy exposure, request serum agglutination test (SAT/Wright's test); a titre ≥1:160 is diagnostic for brucellosis.
keyClue: Undulant fever + night sweats + hepatosplenomegaly + unpasteurised dairy + animal birth contact = brucellosis; confirm with SAT.
commonExamTrap: Requesting only standard 3-day blood cultures — Brucella is slow-growing and standard short-incubation cultures are often falsely negative.
missedConcept: Brucella requires prolonged blood culture incubation (up to 4 weeks); serology (SAT) is faster and more practical for primary care diagnosis.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old male farmer from a rural area is confirmed to have brucellosis based on clinical features and a Wright's agglutination titre of 1:320. There is no evidence of spinal, cardiac, or neurological involvement on clinical assessment. He has no allergies and is otherwise healthy with normal renal and hepatic function. He asks his primary care physician about treatment. Which of the following antibiotic regimens is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 14 days
Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks as monotherapy
Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks PLUS rifampicin 600–900 mg daily for 6 weeks
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 4 weeks as monotherapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole) twice daily for 3 weeks as first-line
explanation: The WHO-recommended first-line treatment for uncomplicated brucellosis is doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 6 weeks combined with rifampicin 600–900 mg daily for 6 weeks. Combination therapy is essential because monotherapy with any single agent has an unacceptably high relapse rate. Rifampicin achieves excellent intracellular penetration against this facultative intracellular pathogen. Doxycycline plus streptomycin is an alternative with lower relapse rates but requires IM injections. Amoxicillin and ciprofloxacin monotherapy are not standard regimens for brucellosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Amoxicillin is not a standard agent in brucellosis treatment regimens; Brucella spp. are intracellular and require agents with good intracellular penetration.
Doxycycline monotherapy for 6 weeks has a high relapse rate (up to 30–40%); combination therapy is mandatory to prevent relapse.
Correct — doxycycline + rifampicin for 6 weeks is the standard WHO-recommended first-line oral combination regimen for uncomplicated brucellosis.
Fluoroquinolone monotherapy is not a recommended first-line regimen; ciprofloxacin may be used in combination regimens but not alone.
Co-trimoxazole monotherapy for 3 weeks is inadequate; it may be used in children but is not the first-line adult regimen, and duration is too short.
clinicalPearl: Brucellosis requires combination antibiotic therapy for 6 weeks; doxycycline + rifampicin is the standard oral regimen — monotherapy causes high relapse rates.
keyClue: Confirmed brucellosis without complications = doxycycline + rifampicin for 6 weeks (WHO first-line).
commonExamTrap: Choosing doxycycline monotherapy — monotherapy for any duration has an unacceptably high relapse rate in brucellosis.
missedConcept: Brucella is an intracellular pathogen; treatment requires agents with intracellular activity (doxycycline, rifampicin, aminoglycosides) given in combination for adequate duration.
